enrichment.short_description: OctoPrint is a free, open-source web interface that lets makers monitor and control their 3D printers remotely from any browser, complete with webcam streaming and a large plugin ecosystem.
enrichment.company_size: Independent open-source project led by a solo maintainer with community contributors
enrichment.seo_title: OctoPrint Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OctoPrint review 2026: explore this free, open-source 3D printer web interface's remote monitoring, plugin ecosystem, webcam support, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OctoPrint is a free and open-source web interface for consumer 3D printers that lets users monitor and control print jobs remotely from a browser or mobile device, instead of tethering a printer directly to a PC. It was created by German developer Gina Haussge, who wrote the first version over a Christmas break in December 2012 after growing frustrated with having to babysit a noisy, fume-producing printer at her desk; she posted the project publicly and put the code on GitHub within days. Haussge went full-time on OctoPrint in 2014 after a Spanish 3D-printer manufacturer hired her to maintain it, and since 2016, when that corporate sponsorship ended, the project has been sustained primarily through community donations via Patreon, where it draws several thousand dollars a month from thousands of backers. OctoPrint typically runs on a Raspberry Pi (via the companion OctoPi disk image) or any Linux, Windows, or macOS machine connected to the printer over USB, and it exposes a dashboard for starting, pausing, and stopping prints, live GCODE visualization, temperature and axis control, webcam streaming, and automated timelapse capture. Its defining feature is a mature plugin system and official plugin repository: thousands of community-built plugins add capabilities like bed-leveling visualization, advanced timelapses (Octolapse), UI themes, printer-specific firmware support, and slicing integration, making OctoPrint the closest thing the desktop 3D-printing world has to a universal, extensible print server. It is released under the GNU Affero General Public License (AGPL), meaning the source is fully open and any hosted or modified version must also share its code, and there is no paid tier, subscription, or official cloud SaaS offering - everything is self-hosted and free. This differentiates OctoPrint from commercial cloud print-management platforms that host printer connectivity for a fee; OctoPrint instead asks users to run their own server, trading convenience for full control, privacy, and no vendor lock-in. It is widely regarded as the de facto standard for remote 3D-printer management among hobbyists, makerspaces, classrooms, and even small print farms, and several printer manufacturers explicitly support or bundle OctoPrint compatibility. Because the project is maintained largely by one core developer, its roadmap and release cadence are tied to community funding and open-source contributions rather than a company's product team, and users needing enterprise support or fleet-management dashboards for large print farms often layer commercial tools like OctoFarm on top of it. For anyone comparing 3D-printer software, OctoPrint's combination of zero cost, deep community plugin support, and printer-agnostic design makes it a common default recommendation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OctoPrint turns a bare 3D printer into a networked device you can watch and operate from anywhere on your local network or, with additional configuration, remotely over the internet. Instead of standing next to a noisy printer or leaving a laptop tethered to it for hours, users install OctoPrint on a small always-on computer, most commonly a Raspberry Pi running the official OctoPi image, and connect it to the printer over USB.

The project began as a personal side project by Gina Haussge in December 2012 and grew into what is now considered the standard open-source web interface for desktop 3D printing, with an active GitHub community, an official plugin repository, and support baked into many commercial printer firmwares and slicers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, OctoPrint provides a live dashboard showing print progress, GCODE visualization, temperature graphs, and full control over printer movement, extrusion, and print jobs (start, pause, cancel). A built-in webcam integration lets users watch prints live and automatically stitch timelapse videos of completed jobs.

The plugin system is what sets OctoPrint apart: thousands of community plugins extend it with features like bed-mesh visualization, advanced timelapse control (Octolapse), custom UI themes, notification integrations, and printer-specific tooling, all installable from an in-app plugin manager without touching the command line.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: OctoPrint itself is completely free and always will be, since it is released under the AGPL open-source license; there is no paid tier, seat limit, or feature gate. Running it does require your own hardware (typically a Raspberry Pi, which costs roughly $35-$80) and, optionally, a webcam.

Because there is no company behind OctoPrint selling licenses, ongoing development is funded through voluntary donations, primarily via Patreon, where thousands of backers collectively support Gina Haussge's full-time work on the project.

features.title: Remote print monitoring and control
features.description: Start, pause, cancel, and track 3D print jobs from any browser on your network, with live GCODE visualization and temperature graphs.

features.title: Webcam streaming and timelapses
features.description: Watch prints live via webcam and automatically generate timelapse videos of completed jobs.

features.title: Extensive plugin ecosystem
features.description: Install thousands of community-built plugins directly from OctoPrint's official plugin repository to add features like bed-leveling visualization, advanced timelapses, and UI themes.

features.title: Printer-agnostic support
features.description: Works with most consumer FDM 3D printers over USB, with community profiles and plugins covering printer-specific quirks.

features.title: Access control and multi-user permissions
features.description: Restrict who can view or control the printer with built-in user accounts and permission levels.

features.title: REST API
features.description: A full REST API lets developers integrate OctoPrint with external dashboards, farm-management tools, and automation scripts.

features.title: Self-hosted and private
features.description: Runs entirely on hardware you control, such as a Raspberry Pi, with no required cloud account or third-party data sharing.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers or feature limits
Massive plugin ecosystem covering nearly every common 3D-printing workflow need
Large, active community and extensive documentation
Works with almost any consumer 3D printer over USB
Full data privacy since everything runs on hardware you control
cons: Requires separate hardware, typically a Raspberry Pi, to run continuously
No official hosted or cloud version, so remote-over-internet access requires extra setup
Initial setup and plugin management can be technical for non-technical makers
Development pace depends on volunteer contributions and Patreon donations rather than a dedicated company roadmap
Managing large print farms requires pairing OctoPrint with additional tools like OctoFarm

faqs.question: Is OctoPrint free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. OctoPrint is free and open-source software released under the AGPL license, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or feature restrictions.

faqs.question: Do I need a Raspberry Pi to run OctoPrint?
faqs.answer: A Raspberry Pi running the official OctoPi image is the most common setup, but OctoPrint can also run on any Linux, Windows, or macOS computer connected to your printer.

faqs.question: Can OctoPrint control any 3D printer?
faqs.answer: OctoPrint works with most consumer FDM printers that connect over USB and speak standard GCODE-based firmware, and the community maintains profiles and plugins for many specific printer models.

faqs.question: How do I install OctoPrint plugins?
faqs.answer: Plugins can be browsed and installed directly from the built-in Plugin Manager inside OctoPrint's web interface, without needing command-line access.

faqs.question: Is it safe to expose OctoPrint to the internet?
faqs.answer: OctoPrint recommends against directly exposing it to the internet without additional protections such as a reverse proxy, VPN, or a dedicated remote-access service, since misconfigured public access has led to reported printer misuse.

faqs.question: Who maintains OctoPrint?
faqs.answer: OctoPrint is maintained primarily by its creator, Gina Haussge, with contributions from an active open-source community, and funded largely through Patreon donations.

faqs.question: What is the difference between OctoPrint and OctoFarm?
faqs.answer: OctoPrint manages a single printer's interface, while OctoFarm is a separate open-source tool that provides a dashboard for monitoring multiple OctoPrint instances at once, useful for print farms.

use_cases.title: Hobbyist remote printer monitoring
use_cases.description: Makers run OctoPrint on a Raspberry Pi next to their printer so they can check progress, watch the webcam feed, and start new jobs from their phone or laptop without being in the same room.

use_cases.title: Classroom and makerspace print management
use_cases.description: Schools and makerspaces use OctoPrint's access controls and web dashboard to let multiple students or members queue and monitor prints on shared printers safely.

use_cases.title: Small print farm operations
use_cases.description: Small businesses running several printers pair OctoPrint on each unit with a farm-management layer like OctoFarm to track jobs, failures, and completion across all machines from one screen.

features: Unlimited use, no seat or feature limits
Self-hosted on your own hardware (e.g. Raspberry Pi)
Community support via forums and GitHub

enrichment.short_description: Odoo Accounting is the bookkeeping and invoicing app inside the Odoo ERP suite, using AI-powered data capture and automated bank reconciliation to handle invoicing, tax reporting, and financial reporting.
enrichment.seo_title: Odoo Accounting Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Odoo Accounting review 2026: AI invoice capture, automated bank reconciliation, and multi-country tax reporting inside Odoo's ERP suite. See pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Odoo Accounting is the finance and bookkeeping application within Odoo, the all-in-one business management suite built by Belgian company Odoo S.A., founded in 2005 by Fabien Pinckaers and headquartered in Ramillies, Belgium. Unlike standalone accounting tools such as QuickBooks or Xero, Odoo Accounting is designed to run as one interconnected app inside a broader ERP: invoices generated from Sales or Inventory automatically post to the ledger, and every payment, bill, or expense flows through the same database as CRM, Inventory, and HR. The app can be adopted on its own, free forever for unlimited users, through Odoo's 'One App Free' plan, or as part of the paid Standard and Custom plans that unlock the full suite of Odoo apps starting at roughly $31 per user per month billed annually. Core capabilities include automated customer invoice generation from sales orders or delivery orders, AI-powered invoice data capture that Odoo states recognizes around 98% of scanned vendor bills without manual entry, and bank reconciliation that connects to more than 28,000 banks worldwide and uses AI matching to auto-reconcile roughly 95% of transactions. Its tax engine supports price-included and price-excluded calculations, tax-on-tax, partial exemptions, and localized tax reports formatted for individual countries' regulatory requirements, with a tax audit view that lets accountants trace how each figure was computed. Beyond day-to-day bookkeeping, the app includes budgeting tools, fixed-asset management, multi-currency and multi-company accounting, and real-time financial dashboards and reports that can be annotated and exported. Odoo Accounting's target audience spans small businesses wanting an affordable, all-in-one alternative to stitching together separate invoicing, CRM, and accounting tools, as well as growing companies and accounting firms managing multiple entities that benefit from Odoo's multi-company consolidation. Its main competitive differentiation versus QuickBooks, Xero, FreshBooks, or Wave is depth of integration: because it shares a database with Odoo's Sales, Inventory, Point of Sale, and Project apps, transactions never need to be re-entered or synced between separate systems. The trade-off is that unlocking Odoo Accounting's full value typically means adopting more of the Odoo ecosystem, and businesses wanting a pure best-of-breed accounting tool with the deepest possible feature set may find dedicated platforms more specialized. As part of Odoo S.A., which employs roughly 5,000 people globally and has raised over $500 million from investors including Sequoia Capital and CapitalG at a valuation above $5 billion, Odoo Accounting benefits from continuous investment in AI features, localizations for over 100 countries, and a large partner network for implementation support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Odoo Accounting handles invoicing, bill payment, bank reconciliation, and financial reporting as a native module of the Odoo ERP platform, meaning transactions created in Sales, Inventory, or Point of Sale post directly to the books without manual re-entry.
It is built for businesses that want accounting to sit inside the same system as the rest of their operations rather than as a disconnected tool that requires constant syncing or double data entry.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: AI-assisted invoice capture reads scanned or emailed vendor bills and pre-fills the relevant fields, while bank reconciliation connects directly to thousands of banks and suggests matches automatically, cutting down the manual work typically associated with month-end closing.

The built-in tax engine handles country-specific tax rules, and real-time financial reports (profit and loss, balance sheet, cash flow, and custom dashboards) update instantly as transactions are recorded elsewhere in Odoo.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Odoo Accounting can be used entirely free, for unlimited users, through Odoo's One App Free plan, which includes hosting and maintenance for that single app. Businesses that also want Sales, Inventory, CRM, or other Odoo apps move to the Standard plan (around $31 per user per month billed annually) or the Custom plan (around $47 per user per month billed annually), which adds Odoo Studio customization, multi-company support, and API access.
Because pricing is per named user across whichever apps are enabled, cost scales with team size rather than transaction volume or invoice count.

features.title: AI-powered invoice data capture
features.description: Automatically extracts and pre-fills data from scanned or emailed vendor bills, reducing manual entry.

features.title: Automated bank reconciliation
features.description: Connects to over 28,000 banks worldwide and uses AI matching to reconcile transactions against invoices and bills automatically.

features.title: Multi-country tax engine
features.description: Supports price-included and price-excluded tax, tax-on-tax, partial exemptions, and localized tax reports for accrual or cash accounting.

features.title: Real-time financial reporting
features.description: Profit and loss, balance sheet, cash flow, and custom dashboards update instantly as transactions post across Odoo.

features.title: Budgeting and asset management
features.description: Track budgets against actuals and manage fixed-asset depreciation schedules from within the same app.

features.title: Multi-currency and multi-company accounting
features.description: Consolidate accounts across multiple currencies and legal entities from a single Odoo database.

features.title: Native integration with Sales, Inventory, and POS
features.description: Invoices, bills, and payments generated elsewhere in Odoo post directly to the ledger without manual syncing.

pros: Deep native integration with Sales, Inventory, CRM, and POS avoids duplicate data entry
AI-powered invoice capture and bank reconciliation cut manual bookkeeping work
Free to start for unlimited users via the One App Free plan
Localized tax and reporting support for 100+ countries
Scales into a full ERP as the business grows, without switching platforms
cons: Full value requires adopting other Odoo apps rather than using Accounting as a pure standalone tool
Per-user pricing on the Standard and Custom plans can add up for larger teams
Migrating historical data from QuickBooks, Xero, or similar tools takes upfront effort
Some advanced compliance and localization features depend on Odoo Enterprise or country-specific packs
Support quality and implementation depth often depend on the Odoo partner handling setup

faqs.question: Is Odoo Accounting free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Odoo Accounting can be used for free with unlimited users through Odoo's One App Free plan; the paid Standard and Custom plans unlock the full suite of Odoo apps alongside it.

faqs.question: Can Odoo Accounting replace QuickBooks or Xero?
faqs.answer: For businesses already using or planning to use other Odoo apps like Sales, Inventory, or CRM, Odoo Accounting can fully replace standalone tools like QuickBooks or Xero, since it shares one database with the rest of the ERP.

faqs.question: Does Odoo Accounting support multiple currencies?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports multi-currency accounting as well as multi-company consolidation for businesses operating across several legal entities.

faqs.question: How accurate is Odoo's AI invoice capture?
faqs.answer: Odoo states its AI-powered invoice recognition correctly captures data from around 98% of scanned or emailed vendor bills, reducing manual entry.

faqs.question: Does Odoo Accounting handle tax compliance for my country?
faqs.answer: Odoo maintains localized tax and accounting rules for more than 100 countries, with tax reports formatted for local regulatory requirements.

faqs.question: How does bank reconciliation work in Odoo?
faqs.answer: Odoo connects directly to over 28,000 banks and uses AI-powered matching to suggest or automatically complete reconciliation between bank statements and recorded invoices or bills.

faqs.question: Who is Odoo Accounting best suited for?
faqs.answer: It suits small and mid-sized businesses that want accounting tightly integrated with sales, inventory, and other operations, as well as accounting firms managing multiple client entities.

use_cases.title: All-in-one bookkeeping for small businesses
use_cases.description: Small businesses use Odoo Accounting to handle invoicing, expenses, and bank reconciliation without paying for a separate accounting subscription.

use_cases.title: Integrated finance for growing companies
use_cases.description: Scaling companies connect Accounting to Odoo Sales, Inventory, and CRM so revenue, stock, and cash flow stay in sync automatically as order volume grows.

use_cases.title: Multi-entity accounting for firms and franchises
use_cases.description: Accounting firms and multi-location businesses use Odoo's multi-company features to consolidate books across several legal entities from one system.

features: Unlimited users on the Accounting app
Access to any apps Accounting depends on at no cost
Full suite of Odoo apps including Accounting
Multi-company support and external API access
Choice of Odoo Online, Odoo.sh, or on-premise hosting

enrichment.short_description: Odoo Community is the free, open-source core edition of the Odoo ERP platform, giving businesses self-hostable access to hundreds of interconnected business apps under the LGPL v3 license.
enrichment.seo_title: Odoo Community Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Odoo Community review 2026: the free, open-source edition of Odoo's ERP suite. See what's included, self-hosting requirements, and how it compares to Enterprise.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Odoo Community is the free and open-source edition of Odoo, the modular business-management platform built by Belgian company Odoo S.A., founded in 2005 by Fabien Pinckaers (originally as TinyERP, renamed OpenERP in 2008, then Odoo in 2014) and headquartered in Ramillies, Belgium. Community is the foundation on which the entire Odoo ecosystem is built: it is released under the LGPL v3 license, meaning anyone can download, install, modify, and redistribute the source code at no cost, with no per-user fees, no artificial feature caps within its scope, and no license enforcement, provided distributed derivatives of the core code remain under the same license. It includes core apps such as Sales, CRM, Inventory, Accounting and Invoicing, Point of Sale, Project, Manufacturing, Website, and HR, all sharing one underlying database and framework so data flows between apps without manual syncing or middleware. Because it is self-hosted, businesses run Community on their own servers or cloud infrastructure and are responsible for installation, upgrades, backups, and ongoing maintenance themselves, or via one of Odoo's thousands of independent implementation partners. Community differs from Odoo Enterprise, the paid, subscription-based edition, primarily in scope and support: Enterprise adds roughly 40 additional modules and capabilities not available in Community, including Odoo Studio (drag-and-drop customization), advanced Helpdesk with SLAs, Field Service, Quality Control, Marketing Automation, Appraisals, Sign, IoT Box integration, multi-company consolidation, official mobile apps, and AI features, plus official Odoo hosting, upgrades, and support. Community's target audience is developers, system integrators, and cost-conscious businesses that want full control over their ERP stack, are comfortable self-hosting or working with a partner to do so, and don't need Enterprise-only modules. It competes less with other open-source ERPs like ERPNext or Dolibarr on raw feature count and more on ecosystem size: Odoo's app store, community, and partner network are among the largest of any open-source business-software project. Because Odoo S.A. itself is a large, well-funded company, having raised more than $500 million from investors including Sequoia Capital and CapitalG at a valuation above $5 billion, Community benefits from steady core-platform development even though the edition itself is free, funded indirectly by Enterprise and Odoo Online subscription revenue. For businesses that outgrow self-hosting or need Enterprise-only apps, Odoo provides a defined upgrade path to Enterprise without needing to migrate off the platform entirely.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Odoo Community is the open-source core of the Odoo platform: the same underlying framework and database model used across every Odoo app, released under the LGPL v3 license so anyone can self-host it for free.

It includes core versions of Odoo's most-used apps, letting businesses run a full, interconnected ERP, covering sales, inventory, accounting, and more, without paying license fees, though hosting and implementation still require time or budget.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Because Community shares Odoo's single-database architecture, installing multiple apps (say, CRM and Inventory) automatically links their data, so a won opportunity can trigger a delivery order without custom integration work.

The LGPL v3 license permits modification and redistribution, which has produced a large ecosystem of community-built apps in the Odoo Apps store, alongside official documentation and an active developer community on GitHub and Odoo's forums.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Odoo Community itself carries no license fee, no per-user cost, and no expiration; the only ongoing costs are hosting infrastructure, implementation, and any development or maintenance work, whether done in-house or through an Odoo partner.

Businesses that want Odoo's official hosting, automatic upgrades, and Enterprise-only modules can move to the paid Standard plan (around $31/user/month billed annually) or Custom plan (around $47/user/month billed annually), which run the Enterprise edition instead of Community.

features.title: LGPL v3 open-source license
features.description: Full source code access with the freedom to use, modify, and redistribute it, with no per-user or per-app licensing fees.

features.title: Modular app architecture
features.description: Install only the apps you need, such as Sales, Inventory, Accounting, or CRM, all sharing one underlying database.

features.title: Self-hosting freedom
features.description: Deploy on your own servers or any cloud provider, giving full control over data location, backups, and infrastructure.

features.title: Large community app store
features.description: Thousands of community-built modules extend Community beyond the core apps, covering industry-specific and niche needs.

features.title: Developer and API extensibility
features.description: Full access to Odoo's Python-based framework and APIs for custom development and integrations.

features.title: Active open-source community
features.description: Public GitHub repositories, forums, and a large partner network provide support outside of official Odoo channels.

features.title: Clear upgrade path to Enterprise
features.description: Businesses that outgrow Community's scope can move to Odoo Enterprise without abandoning their existing data or setup.

pros: No license fees or per-user costs, ever
Full source code access with freedom to modify and self-host
Large, mature ecosystem of apps, partners, and documentation
Modular design lets you run only the apps your business needs
No vendor lock-in since the code and data stay under your control
cons: Lacks Enterprise-only modules like Studio, Field Service, and advanced Helpdesk
Self-hosting requires technical resources for setup, security, and upgrades
No official Odoo support or SLA is included, only community or partner support
Major version upgrades can require manual data migration work
No official mobile app or IoT Box integration available in this edition

faqs.question: Is Odoo Community really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Odoo Community is released under the LGPL v3 open-source license and can be downloaded, self-hosted, and used with no license fees, though hosting and implementation still cost time or money.

faqs.question: What's the difference between Odoo Community and Odoo Enterprise?
faqs.answer: Enterprise adds roughly 40 extra modules and features not in Community, such as Odoo Studio, advanced Helpdesk, Field Service, multi-company consolidation, and official mobile apps, plus official hosting, upgrades, and support.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Odoo Community?
faqs.answer: Yes, Community is designed to be self-hosted on your own servers or any cloud provider; Odoo also has an on-premise installation guide and a large partner network that can host or manage it for you.

faqs.question: Can I upgrade from Community to Enterprise later?
faqs.answer: Yes, Odoo provides a defined migration path from Community to Enterprise for businesses that need Enterprise-only apps or official support as they grow.

faqs.question: What apps are included in Odoo Community?
faqs.answer: Community includes core versions of apps like Sales, CRM, Inventory, Accounting and Invoicing, Point of Sale, Project, Manufacturing, Website, and HR, among others.

faqs.question: Is Odoo Community good for small businesses?
faqs.answer: It's a strong fit for small businesses with in-house technical resources or an implementation partner, since it offers a full ERP at no license cost, though very small teams without IT support may prefer the hosted Standard plan instead.

faqs.question: What does the LGPL v3 license actually require?
faqs.answer: LGPL v3 lets you use, modify, and distribute Odoo Community freely, and any distributed derivative of the core Community code must remain under the same license, though this does not extend to separate Enterprise-only modules.

use_cases.title: Budget-conscious ERP for technical teams
use_cases.description: Businesses with in-house developers self-host Odoo Community to run a full ERP without paying per-user license fees.
use_cases.audience: Startups and SMBs with technical staff

use_cases.title: Custom ERP builds by system integrators
use_cases.description: Odoo partners and system integrators use Community's open codebase as a foundation for heavily customized, industry-specific ERP deployments.

use_cases.title: Proof-of-concept before committing to Enterprise
use_cases.description: Companies evaluate Odoo by self-hosting Community first, testing workflows before deciding whether to move to the paid Enterprise edition for official support and extra modules.

features: Full source code under LGPL v3
Core apps including Sales, CRM, Inventory, Accounting, and POS
Odoo Studio, multi-company support, API access
Odoo Online, Odoo.sh, or on-premise hosting options

enrichment.short_description: Odoo CRM is Odoo's sales pipeline and customer relationship module, combining a visual Kanban pipeline, predictive lead scoring, and built-in forecasting to help sales teams track and close deals.
enrichment.seo_title: Odoo CRM Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Odoo CRM review 2026: visual pipeline management, predictive lead scoring, and sales forecasting built into Odoo's ERP suite. Compare pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Odoo CRM is the customer relationship management app within Odoo, the modular business platform built by Odoo S.A., a Belgian company founded in 2005 by Fabien Pinckaers and headquartered in Ramillies, Belgium. Unlike standalone CRMs such as Salesforce or HubSpot, Odoo CRM is designed to operate as one connected app inside a broader ERP, so a won opportunity can automatically generate a quote, sales order, stock reservation, and invoice without leaving the record or re-entering data elsewhere. It can be used entirely on its own and for free, for unlimited users, via Odoo's 'One App Free' plan, or as part of the paid Standard (roughly $31/user/month billed annually) and Custom (roughly $47/user/month billed annually) plans that unlock the rest of Odoo's apps alongside it. Its core is a drag-and-drop Kanban pipeline where opportunities move through customizable stages, each showing win probability and time spent in stage, rolling up automatically into weighted sales forecasts. Odoo CRM includes predictive lead scoring, a machine-learning model trained on a company's historical CRM data that estimates the probability of winning each new lead or opportunity, which can drive automated lead assignment by score, territory, or round robin. Leads can be auto-captured from website forms, email aliases, live chat conversations, and even scanned trade-show business cards or CSV imports, then routed with configurable assignment rules. Every call, email, and meeting logged against a contact builds a complete activity history, and reporting tools include pipeline analysis, win and loss tracking, and revenue forecasting by expected close date. Odoo CRM's target audience is small and mid-sized sales teams, especially those already using or planning to use other Odoo apps like Sales, Accounting, or Marketing, since the payoff of tighter integration compounds as more of the suite is adopted. Its main competitive trade-off against dedicated CRM platforms like Salesforce, HubSpot, or Pipedrive is that Odoo CRM's marketing automation and third-party app-marketplace depth are less extensive than CRM-specialist platforms, but it wins on total cost of ownership and integration depth for companies that want quoting, invoicing, and inventory to update automatically as deals close. As part of Odoo S.A., a company employing roughly 5,000 people globally and having raised more than $500 million in funding at a valuation above $5 billion, Odoo CRM benefits from continued investment in AI-driven lead scoring and forecasting features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Odoo CRM organizes a sales team's pipeline into a visual, drag-and-drop Kanban board where opportunities move through configurable stages from first contact to closed-won, with win probability and time-in-stage tracked automatically.

Because it's built on the same database as the rest of Odoo, a closed deal in Odoo CRM can trigger a quote, sales order, and invoice automatically, connecting sales activity directly to fulfillment and accounting without manual handoffs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Predictive lead scoring uses a machine-learning model trained on historical CRM data to estimate each lead's probability of closing, which teams can use to prioritize follow-up or drive automated lead-assignment rules by score, territory, or round robin.

Leads are captured automatically from website forms, email aliases, live chat, and trade-show imports, while every call, email, and meeting logged against a contact builds a full activity timeline that supports pipeline and win-loss reporting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Odoo CRM can be used for free with unlimited users through the One App Free plan. Businesses that also want Odoo's Sales, Marketing, or Accounting apps move to the Standard plan (around $31/user/month billed annually) or Custom plan (around $47/user/month billed annually).

Pricing is per named user across whichever Odoo apps are active, so a sales team using only CRM pays nothing beyond the free tier, while teams adopting the full suite pay per seat for the whole bundle.

features.title: Visual Kanban pipeline
features.description: Drag-and-drop opportunities through customizable stages with win probability and time-in-stage tracked automatically.

features.title: Predictive lead scoring
features.description: A machine-learning model trained on historical CRM data estimates each lead's likelihood of closing to help prioritize follow-up.

features.title: Automated lead capture
features.description: Captures leads from website forms, email aliases, live chat conversations, and trade-show CSV imports.

features.title: Sales forecasting
features.description: Pipeline value rolls up automatically by expected close date and stage probability for revenue forecasting.

features.title: Activity tracking
features.description: Logs every call, email, and meeting against a contact or opportunity to build a complete interaction history.

features.title: One-click quote-to-cash
features.description: Converts opportunities into quotes, then sales orders, stock reservations, and invoices without leaving the record.

features.title: Lead assignment rules
features.description: Automatically route leads to reps based on score, territory, or round-robin logic.

pros: Closed deals can automatically trigger quotes, orders, and invoices without re-entering data
Predictive lead scoring is built in at no extra cost
Free entry point for small sales teams via the One App Free plan
Highly customizable pipeline stages and views
Scales naturally into a full ERP as a business adopts more Odoo apps
cons: Marketing automation depth trails CRM-specialist platforms like HubSpot
Third-party app marketplace and integrations are less extensive than Salesforce's ecosystem
Mobile CRM experience is less polished than dedicated mobile-first CRMs
Initial pipeline and automation setup takes configuration time
Full benefits require adopting other Odoo apps rather than using CRM in isolation

faqs.question: Is Odoo CRM free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Odoo CRM can be used for free with unlimited users through Odoo's One App Free plan; the paid Standard and Custom plans add the rest of Odoo's apps alongside it.

faqs.question: Does Odoo CRM include lead scoring?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes predictive lead scoring, a machine-learning model trained on historical CRM data that estimates each lead's probability of converting.

faqs.question: Can Odoo CRM automatically create invoices from closed deals?
faqs.answer: Yes, because CRM shares a database with Odoo's Sales and Accounting apps, a won opportunity can trigger a quote, sales order, and invoice automatically.

faqs.question: How does Odoo CRM capture leads?
faqs.answer: Leads are captured automatically from website forms, dedicated email aliases, live chat conversations, and CSV imports from events or trade shows.

faqs.question: Is Odoo CRM good for small sales teams?
faqs.answer: Yes, small teams can start on the free One App Free plan, though the value grows as a business connects CRM to other Odoo apps like Sales and Marketing.

faqs.question: How does Odoo CRM compare to Salesforce or HubSpot?
faqs.answer: Odoo CRM offers less marketing-automation and app-marketplace depth than CRM specialists like Salesforce or HubSpot, but provides tighter native integration with quoting, invoicing, and inventory at a lower total cost.

faqs.question: Can I customize the sales pipeline stages?
faqs.answer: Yes, pipeline stages, fields, and views are fully customizable to match a team's specific sales process.

use_cases.title: Pipeline management for small sales teams
use_cases.description: Sales reps track opportunities through a visual pipeline, log activities, and get automated forecasts without needing a separate CRM subscription.

use_cases.title: Quote-to-cash automation
use_cases.description: Sales teams already using Odoo Sales and Accounting close deals in CRM that automatically generate quotes, orders, and invoices.
use_cases.audience: Sales teams using multiple Odoo apps

use_cases.title: Multi-pipeline agency and franchise management
use_cases.description: Agencies and franchise networks manage separate pipelines per client or location while keeping reporting centralized in one Odoo database.

features: Unlimited users on the CRM app
Access to any apps CRM depends on at no cost
Full suite of Odoo apps including CRM
Multi-company support and external API access
Choice of Odoo Online, Odoo.sh, or on-premise hosting

enrichment.short_description: Odoo Point of Sale is a cloud-connected checkout app for retail shops and restaurants that syncs sales with real-time inventory, runs offline, and works on iPad, Android, or browser-based terminals.
enrichment.seo_title: Odoo Point of Sale Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Odoo Point of Sale review 2026: offline-capable retail and restaurant checkout with real-time inventory sync. See features, pricing, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Odoo Point of Sale (POS) is the retail and restaurant checkout app within Odoo, the modular ERP platform built by Belgian company Odoo S.A., founded in 2005 by Fabien Pinckaers and headquartered in Ramillies, Belgium. Unlike standalone POS systems such as Square or Toast, Odoo POS runs as a connected app inside Odoo's broader ERP, meaning every sale updates inventory, accounting, and customer records in real time within the same database used by Odoo's Sales, Inventory, and Accounting apps. It can be adopted on its own for free, with unlimited users, through Odoo's One App Free plan, or as part of the paid Standard (roughly $31/user/month billed annually) and Custom (roughly $47/user/month billed annually) plans that add the rest of Odoo's app suite. The app runs on iPads, Android tablets, or any browser-based terminal, and is designed to keep working during internet outages: it processes sales offline and automatically syncs everything, transactions, inventory changes, and customer data, once connectivity returns. For retail, it supports barcode scanning with embedded pricing, weight, and loyalty data, multi-store and multi-register management, and loyalty programs or gift cards. For restaurants and cafes, it adds course-by-course order timing, kitchen and bar printer routing, table management, and split-bill support, letting staff send appetizers to the kitchen immediately while holding mains for a later fire time. Hardware such as receipt printers, barcode scanners, cash drawers, and card readers typically connect through Odoo's IoT Box, and receipts themselves can be customized to promote current offers or upcoming events. Because inventory updates in real time as sales are rung up, businesses avoid the stock discrepancies common when POS and warehouse systems run separately. Odoo POS's target audience spans independent retail stores, restaurants, cafes, and pop-up or market vendors that want checkout tightly connected to inventory and accounting without paying for and integrating three separate systems. Its main trade-off against POS specialists like Square, Toast, or Lightspeed is that achieving full hardware compatibility and advanced restaurant workflows often requires more setup and Odoo-specific hardware (the IoT Box) rather than plug-and-play consumer devices, but it wins on integration depth and total cost for businesses already using or planning to use Odoo for the rest of their operations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Odoo Point of Sale is a checkout system built to run on tablets, browsers, or dedicated terminals in retail stores and restaurants, designed from the ground up to share live data with the rest of the Odoo ERP rather than operating as an isolated till.
Every transaction rung up in Odoo POS updates inventory counts, customer records, and accounting entries in real time, so store, warehouse, and finance teams are always looking at the same numbers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The app is built to keep working when the internet drops: sales, inventory adjustments, and customer updates continue offline and sync automatically once the connection is restored, which matters for retail floors and busy restaurant services where downtime isn't an option.

Restaurant-specific tools let staff fire courses to the kitchen or bar at different times, manage table layouts, and split bills, while retail-specific tools handle barcode scanning with embedded pricing and loyalty data, multi-store inventory, and gift cards.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Odoo POS can be used for free with unlimited users on the One App Free plan. Adding Odoo's Inventory, Accounting, or other apps moves a business to the Standard plan (around $31/user/month billed annually) or Custom plan (around $47/user/month billed annually).
Hardware, such as receipt printers, barcode scanners, and card readers, is a separate cost from the software subscription and typically connects through Odoo's IoT Box for full compatibility.

features.title: Real-time inventory sync
features.description: Every sale immediately updates stock levels across the connected Odoo Inventory app, across single or multiple store locations.

features.title: Offline mode with auto-sync
features.description: Continues processing sales and inventory changes without an internet connection, syncing automatically once reconnected.

features.title: Restaurant course and kitchen routing
features.description: Sends order items to kitchen or bar printers at different times per course, with table management and bill splitting.

features.title: Barcode scanning with embedded data
features.description: Scans barcodes that embed price, weight, or loyalty information for fast, accurate checkout.

features.title: Multi-store and multi-register management
features.description: Runs multiple registers and locations from a single Odoo database with centralized reporting.

features.title: Loyalty programs and gift cards
features.description: Built-in tools to run point-based loyalty programs and issue or redeem gift cards at checkout.

features.title: IoT Box hardware support
features.description: Connects receipt printers, scales, cash drawers, and card readers over the local network via Odoo's IoT Box.

pros: Real-time inventory sync eliminates stock discrepancies between POS and warehouse
Works offline and syncs automatically, useful for unreliable internet connections
Free entry point with unlimited users via the One App Free plan
Handles both retail and restaurant workflows in one system
Runs on affordable, widely available hardware like iPads and Android tablets
cons: Full hardware compatibility (printers, cash drawers, card readers) often requires Odoo's IoT Box
Advanced restaurant features like course firing and table maps require initial configuration
Per-user costs on Standard and Custom plans can add up for businesses with many registers or staff
Some niche retail features, like complex tiered loyalty programs, may need Enterprise-only apps
Initial setup requires familiarity with the Odoo platform rather than being fully plug-and-play

faqs.question: Is Odoo Point of Sale free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can be used for free with unlimited users through Odoo's One App Free plan; adding other Odoo apps requires the paid Standard or Custom plan.

faqs.question: Does Odoo POS work without internet?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's designed to keep processing sales, inventory changes, and customer data offline, then automatically syncs everything once the connection returns.

faqs.question: What hardware does Odoo POS support?
faqs.answer: It supports iPads, Android tablets, and browser-based terminals, with receipt printers, scales, cash drawers, and card readers typically connected through Odoo's IoT Box.

faqs.question: Can Odoo POS handle restaurants as well as retail?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes restaurant-specific tools like course-by-course kitchen and bar printer routing, table management, and bill splitting, alongside retail tools like barcode scanning and multi-store inventory.

faqs.question: Does Odoo POS update inventory automatically?
faqs.answer: Yes, every sale updates stock levels in real time across the connected Odoo Inventory app, whether for a single store or multiple locations.

faqs.question: Does Odoo POS support loyalty programs and gift cards?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes built-in point-based loyalty programs and gift card issuance and redemption at checkout.

faqs.question: How does Odoo POS compare to Square or Toast?
faqs.answer: Odoo POS offers deeper native integration with inventory and accounting for businesses already on Odoo, while Square and Toast offer more plug-and-play hardware and payment processing specifically tuned for retail or restaurants respectively.

use_cases.title: Independent retail store checkout
use_cases.description: Small retail shops use Odoo POS to ring up sales on iPads while inventory updates automatically in the back office.

use_cases.title: Restaurant and cafe order management
use_cases.description: Restaurants use course-by-course kitchen routing and table management to coordinate front-of-house and kitchen staff during service.

use_cases.title: Pop-up shops and market vendors
use_cases.description: Vendors at markets or pop-up events use Odoo POS's offline mode to keep selling without reliable internet, syncing sales once back online.

features: Unlimited users on the Point of Sale app
Access to any apps POS depends on at no cost
Full suite of Odoo apps including Point of Sale
Multi-company support and external API access
Choice of Odoo Online, Odoo.sh, or on-premise hosting

enrichment.short_description: Offen is an open-source, privacy-first web analytics tool that collects usage data only with visitor consent and gives both site owners and visitors transparent access to the same data.
enrichment.seo_title: Offen Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Offen review 2026: this open-source, privacy-first web analytics tool uses opt-in consent and end-to-end encryption. See features, self-hosting, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Offen is an open-source, self-hosted web analytics tool built around the idea of 'fair' analytics: rather than tracking visitors invisibly, it asks for explicit opt-in consent and then gives visitors the same access to their own collected data that the site operator has. It was created by German developers Frederik Ring and Hendrik Niefeld, who began building the core analytics engine in 2019, with support from the NLnet Foundation's Next Generation Internet initiative funding much of the early development. Over time the project expanded into a small suite of related tools, including the Offen Consent Tool for consent management and a proposed 'analytics.txt' standard for publicly disclosing what analytics software a site uses, all reaching maturity by summer 2024, at which point the founders formalized their work under offen.software, a Berlin-based development studio. Offen is entirely free and open source, distributed under an open license with the full codebase available on GitHub, and can be self-hosted by downloading a single compiled binary or running it as a Docker container; there is no hosted SaaS version and no paid tier. Its core differentiator versus mainstream analytics tools like Google Analytics, or even other privacy-focused alternatives like Plausible or Fathom, is its consent model: rather than collecting data by default and offering an opt-out, Offen collects nothing until a visitor actively opts in, and every visitor who does can then log in and see exactly what data has been collected about their own sessions, with all data encrypted end-to-end. It uses first-party cookies only, avoids third-party trackers or ad-network data sharing entirely, and is designed to comply readily with GDPR and similar privacy regulations by construction rather than through after-the-fact configuration. The interface and documentation are available in English, French, German, Portuguese, Spanish, and Vietnamese. Offen's target audience is privacy-conscious website owners, particularly in Europe, who want basic traffic analytics (pageviews, referrers, popular content) without compromising on consent-based, transparent data practices, as well as developers and agencies building for GDPR-sensitive clients. Its trade-off versus more feature-rich commercial or open-source analytics platforms is scope: because Offen intentionally collects only what visitors consent to share, and does not offer a managed hosting option, it suits teams comfortable running lightweight self-hosted infrastructure and who prioritize transparency over the deepest possible funnel or behavioral analytics.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Offen is a self-hosted, open-source alternative to Google Analytics built around explicit visitor consent: instead of tracking everyone by default, it only records data from visitors who actively opt in, and those visitors can then view exactly what was collected about them.

The project was started in 2019 by German developers Frederik Ring and Hendrik Niefeld and has since grown into a small suite of related privacy tools, including a standalone consent manager, all released as open source and maintained under their studio, offen.software, based in Berlin.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Offen tracks pageviews, referrers, and basic usage metrics using first-party cookies only, with all stored data encrypted end-to-end and no data shared with third parties or ad networks.

Because it's self-hosted, site owners deploy it via a single downloadable binary or a Docker image on their own infrastructure, giving full control over where data lives, which matters for teams with strict data-residency or GDPR requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Offen is completely free with no paid tier, since it is open-source software distributed for self-hosting rather than sold as a hosted service. Costs are limited to whatever server infrastructure you choose to run it on.

Ongoing development has been supported through grants, notably from the NLnet Foundation, rather than subscription revenue, which keeps the tool free but means release pace depends on the small founding team's capacity and funding.

features.title: Opt-in-only data collection
features.description: No usage data is collected until a visitor actively consents, unlike default-on tracking used by most analytics tools.

features.title: Visitor-facing data access
features.description: Visitors who opt in can log in and see exactly what data has been collected about their own sessions.

features.title: End-to-end encrypted storage
features.description: All collected analytics data is encrypted end-to-end rather than stored in plain, queryable form.

features.title: First-party cookies only
features.description: No third-party trackers or cross-site cookies, reducing exposure to ad-network data sharing.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Runs as a single binary or Docker container on infrastructure you control, with no required third-party hosting.

features.title: GDPR-oriented design
features.description: Consent-first collection and data minimization are built into the architecture rather than bolted on afterward.

features.title: Multi-language support
features.description: Available in English, French, German, Portuguese, Spanish, and Vietnamese.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tier
Consent-first model is genuinely privacy-respecting rather than opt-out-based
Visitors get transparent access to their own collected data
End-to-end encryption of stored analytics data
Lightweight and simple to self-host via Docker or a single binary
cons: No hosted SaaS option, so self-hosting infrastructure is required
Opt-in-only consent naturally reduces the volume of data collected compared to opt-out tools
Smaller community and plugin ecosystem than Google Analytics or Plausible
Fewer built-in integrations with marketing and ad platforms
Development pace depends on a small founding team and grant funding rather than a commercial roadmap

faqs.question: Is Offen free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Offen is fully open source and free, distributed for self-hosting with no paid tier or hosted SaaS option.

faqs.question: How is Offen different from Google Analytics?
faqs.answer: Offen only collects data from visitors who actively opt in, encrypts stored data end-to-end, and lets visitors see their own collected data, whereas Google Analytics tracks by default and doesn't expose raw visitor-level data back to users.

faqs.question: Who built Offen?
faqs.answer: Offen was created by German developers Frederik Ring and Hendrik Niefeld, who started the project in 2019 and now maintain it under their Berlin-based studio, offen.software.

faqs.question: How do I install Offen?
faqs.answer: Offen can be deployed by downloading a single compiled binary or running the official Docker image on your own server or cloud infrastructure.

faqs.question: Is Offen GDPR compliant?
faqs.answer: Offen is designed around opt-in consent, data minimization, and first-party-only cookies, which aligns closely with GDPR principles, though compliance also depends on how it's configured and disclosed on your site.

faqs.question: What languages does Offen support?
faqs.answer: The interface is available in English, French, German, Portuguese, Spanish, and Vietnamese.

faqs.question: Does Offen offer a hosted version?
faqs.answer: No, Offen is self-hosted only; there is no official managed hosting or SaaS plan.

use_cases.title: Privacy-first analytics for EU websites
use_cases.description: European businesses use Offen to get basic traffic insights while meeting GDPR consent requirements by design.

use_cases.title: Transparent analytics for privacy-focused products
use_cases.description: Privacy-conscious startups and open-source projects adopt Offen to align their analytics practices with their broader privacy commitments.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted analytics for data-sovereignty needs
use_cases.description: Organizations that must keep analytics data on infrastructure they control, such as those in regulated industries, self-host Offen instead of using third-party cloud analytics.

enrichment.short_description: Officevibe, now part of Workleap, is an employee engagement platform that uses pulse surveys, eNPS tracking, and anonymous feedback to help managers monitor morale and act on it.
enrichment.seo_title: Officevibe Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Officevibe review 2026: pulse surveys, eNPS, and anonymous feedback for employee engagement, now part of Workleap. See features, pricing, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Officevibe is an employee engagement and pulse-survey platform originally launched in 2013 by GSoft, a Montreal, Canada-based software company founded in 2006 by Simon De Baene, Guillaume Roy, and Sebastien Leduc. GSoft rebranded its entire product portfolio, including Officevibe, under the name Workleap in 2023, positioning Officevibe as its dedicated employee-engagement and feedback product within a broader talent-management suite that also includes performance management, skills, and onboarding tools. Officevibe works by sending short, recurring pulse surveys to employees, tracking metrics like employee Net Promoter Score (eNPS) over time, and collecting anonymous feedback that employees can submit without fear of it being traced back to them, giving managers a continuous read on team morale rather than relying solely on annual engagement surveys. The platform layers in 'Good Vibes,' a peer-to-peer recognition feature for celebrating wins and giving public kudos, and uses AI to generate highlights and trend summaries from survey results, suggest recommended actions for managers based on the data, and help managers draft thoughtful responses to employee feedback. Historically Officevibe sold tiered Essential and Pro plans priced between roughly $3.50 and $7.50 per employee per month depending on billing terms, but Workleap simplified this in 2024 into a single unified plan priced at $5 per user per month billed annually (or $6.25 per user per month billed monthly), with a 10-user minimum. Officevibe integrates with common workplace tools including Slack, Microsoft Teams, and various HRIS platforms for automatic employee provisioning. Its target audience is small and mid-sized businesses, roughly 10 to 200 employees, where a lightweight, easy-to-deploy engagement tool that a single HR or people-ops person can manage is more valuable than a heavyweight enterprise HR suite. Compared to broader HR platforms like Culture Amp, Lattice, or 15Five, Officevibe differentiates on simplicity and speed of setup rather than depth of performance-management or compensation features, which live in Workleap's separate, higher-priced platform tiers (Standard at $4,999/year and Pro at $11,999/year) for organizations wanting a fuller HR suite. Workleap, the parent company, is headquartered in Montreal and reports over 70,000 people leaders using its products globally, with roughly 400 employees of its own.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Officevibe is built around continuous listening rather than a once-a-year engagement survey: short pulse surveys go out on a recurring basis, and results feed a running employee Net Promoter Score (eNPS) that managers can track over time.

Originally launched in 2013 by Montreal-based GSoft, the product has been part of Workleap (GSoft's 2023 rebrand) since then, sitting alongside Workleap's broader performance-management and skills tools as the company's dedicated engagement and pulse-survey product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Employees can submit anonymous feedback at any time, not just during a survey cycle, and managers get AI-generated highlights and suggested actions summarizing what the data is showing without needing to manually dig through raw responses.

'Good Vibes,' Officevibe's peer recognition feature, lets team members send public appreciation cards, while integrations with Slack, Microsoft Teams, and HRIS platforms keep survey participation and employee data in sync with the tools teams already use daily.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Officevibe uses a single, simplified pricing tier as of 2024: $5 per user per month billed annually, or $6.25 per user per month billed monthly, with a 10-user minimum, replacing the previous Essential and Pro tiered structure.

This is separate from Workleap's broader HR platform pricing (Standard at $4,999/year and Pro at $11,999/year), which bundles performance management and compensation planning alongside engagement for organizations wanting a fuller HR suite.

features.title: Recurring pulse surveys
features.description: Short, automated surveys sent on a regular cadence to track engagement trends over time rather than once a year.

features.title: Employee Net Promoter Score (eNPS) tracking
features.description: Continuously measures eNPS so managers can see engagement trends rise or fall in near real time.

features.title: Anonymous feedback channel
features.description: Employees can submit feedback anonymously at any time, encouraging candor without fear of attribution.

features.title: Good Vibes peer recognition
features.description: Lets team members send public recognition cards to celebrate wins and build appreciation culture.

features.title: AI-powered survey reporting
features.description: Automatically generates highlights, trend summaries, and recommended actions from survey results.

features.title: Feedback reply assistance
features.description: AI helps managers draft thoughtful, constructive responses to employee feedback.

features.title: HRIS and workplace integrations
features.description: Connects with Slack, Microsoft Teams, and HRIS platforms for automatic employee provisioning and notifications.

pros: Simple, transparent pricing at $5/user/month with no tiered feature gating
Continuous pulse surveys give a more current read on morale than annual surveys
Anonymous feedback channel encourages honest input
AI-generated highlights reduce the manual work of interpreting survey data
Easy for a single HR or people-ops person to deploy and manage
cons: 10-user minimum means very small teams pay for seats they may not need
Less depth in performance management and compensation than Workleap's higher-tier HR platform
Limited standalone use outside of the Workleap ecosystem for broader HR needs
Fewer advanced customization options than dedicated enterprise engagement platforms like Culture Amp
Historical pricing changes from tiered to unified may require re-budgeting for long-time customers

faqs.question: What is Officevibe used for?
faqs.answer: Officevibe is an employee engagement platform that uses recurring pulse surveys, eNPS tracking, and anonymous feedback to help managers monitor and improve team morale.

faqs.question: Is Officevibe part of Workleap?
faqs.answer: Yes, Officevibe was originally built by GSoft, a Montreal software company founded in 2006, which rebranded as Workleap in 2023; Officevibe remains a core Workleap product.

faqs.question: How much does Officevibe cost?
faqs.answer: Officevibe is priced at $5 per user per month billed annually, or $6.25 per user per month billed monthly, with a 10-user minimum.

faqs.question: Is Officevibe feedback really anonymous?
faqs.answer: Yes, employees can submit feedback anonymously, and Officevibe is designed so responses can't be traced back to individual employees.

faqs.question: Does Officevibe integrate with Slack or Microsoft Teams?
faqs.answer: Yes, Officevibe integrates with Slack, Microsoft Teams, and various HRIS platforms for survey distribution and employee provisioning.

faqs.question: What size company is Officevibe best for?
faqs.answer: Officevibe is best suited to small and mid-sized businesses, roughly 10 to 200 employees, that want a lightweight, easy-to-manage engagement tool.

faqs.question: How is Officevibe different from Culture Amp or Lattice?
faqs.answer: Officevibe focuses specifically on lightweight, continuous engagement surveys and recognition, while platforms like Culture Amp or Lattice offer deeper performance-management and compensation features, often at a higher price point.

use_cases.title: Continuous engagement monitoring
use_cases.description: HR teams use recurring pulse surveys and eNPS tracking to catch morale issues early instead of waiting for an annual survey.
use_cases.audience: HR and people-ops teams at SMBs

use_cases.title: Manager coaching with AI-generated insights
use_cases.description: People managers use AI-generated highlights and recommended actions to know what to address after each survey cycle without manually analyzing raw data.

use_cases.title: Peer recognition culture building
use_cases.description: Teams use Good Vibes recognition cards to reinforce a culture of appreciation alongside formal engagement measurement.

enrichment.short_description: Oh Dear is an all-in-one website monitoring tool, built by two Belgian developers, that tracks uptime, SSL certificates, performance, broken links, DNS, and scheduled tasks from a single dashboard.
enrichment.seo_title: Oh Dear Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Oh Dear review 2026: uptime, SSL, performance, and cron monitoring in one dashboard, built by two Belgian developers. See pricing plans and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Oh Dear is an all-in-one website and server monitoring tool founded in 2018 by Freek Van der Herten, a Belgian agency owner and prolific open-source PHP and Laravel developer, and Mattias Geniar, a sysadmin and team lead at the Belgian hosting company Nucleus. The pairing was deliberate: Van der Herten focused on the developer- and application-facing side of monitoring, while Geniar brought infrastructure, DNS, and security expertise, and their stated goal was to build a tool that covers 'the whole site, not just the half you think about,' combining checks that developers and operations teams traditionally split across separate tools. Rather than offering just uptime pings, Oh Dear bundles uptime monitoring, SSL certificate expiration tracking, broken-link checking, performance and Lighthouse-based audits, DNS record and domain-expiration monitoring, scheduled task (cron) monitoring, ping and TCP port checks, application health checks, and public status pages into one product, and every plan includes every feature; the only thing that changes between pricing tiers is how many sites or servers are covered. Pricing starts at €15 per month for the Solo plan (2 sites) and scales through Freelance (€49/month, 10 sites), Studio (€99/month, 25 sites), Agency (€149/month, 50 sites), Agency Plus (€199/month, 75 sites), Portfolio (€249/month, 100 sites), Portfolio Plus (€329/month, 150 sites), and Scale (€399/month, 200 sites), with custom Enterprise pricing above that; annual billing gets two months free, and there's a 10-day free trial with no credit card required. Since the company began in the Laravel and PHP developer community, where both founders are well known, Oh Dear has built strong brand trust among freelance developers and small agencies monitoring client sites, positioning it as a European, developer-friendly alternative to tools like Pingdom, UptimeRobot, or StatusCake, which often gate features like SSL monitoring or status pages behind higher tiers. Its target audience is freelance developers, digital agencies, and small-to-mid-size SaaS companies that want a single dashboard covering uptime, performance, and infrastructure health for a portfolio of client or product sites, rather than piecing together several point-solution monitoring tools. Being a small, founder-led company rather than a venture-backed startup, Oh Dear has grown its feature set steadily since 2018 through direct founder involvement and continues to add capabilities like AI-powered checks and DNS blocklist monitoring.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Oh Dear was built to solve a problem its two founders kept running into from opposite sides: Freek Van der Herten, a developer, worried about whether a site's application layer and content were working correctly, while Mattias Geniar, a sysadmin, worried about servers, DNS, and infrastructure staying up. Oh Dear combines both perspectives into one monitoring dashboard.

Rather than being a single-purpose uptime pinger, it bundles website and infrastructure checks that are normally split across several tools, uptime, SSL, performance, broken links, DNS, and scheduled tasks, into one product with one price per site.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Every Oh Dear plan includes the full feature set: uptime monitoring, SSL certificate expiration alerts, broken-link scanning, Lighthouse-based performance audits, DNS record and domain-expiration monitoring, scheduled task (cron) monitoring, ping and TCP port checks, and public status pages.

Because there are no feature-gated tiers, single sign-on, two-factor authentication, unlimited users, and status pages are available even on the entry-level Solo plan; only the number of monitored sites or servers changes as you move up in price.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Oh Dear's pricing scales purely by the number of sites or servers monitored rather than by feature access, starting at €15/month for 2 sites (Solo) and rising through several tiers up to €399/month for 200 sites (Scale), with custom Enterprise pricing above that.
Annual billing includes two months free compared to paying monthly, and new users get a 10-day free trial with no credit card required, plus a 30-day money-back guarantee.

features.title: Uptime monitoring
features.description: Checks site availability and alerts the team the moment a site or server goes down.

features.title: SSL certificate monitoring
features.description: Tracks SSL certificate validity and warns before certificates expire or misconfigure.

features.title: Broken link and mixed-content checking
features.description: Scans sites for broken links and mixed-content issues that can hurt SEO and user trust.

features.title: Performance and Lighthouse audits
features.description: Runs Lighthouse-based performance checks to catch page-speed regressions over time.

features.title: DNS and domain-expiration monitoring
features.description: Watches DNS records for unexpected changes and alerts before domain registrations expire.

features.title: Scheduled task (cron) monitoring
features.description: Confirms that scheduled jobs and cron tasks actually run on time, not just that the server is up.

features.title: Public status pages
features.description: Publishes a branded status page that subscribers can follow for incident and uptime updates.

pros: Every plan includes the full feature set, no functionality is gated behind higher tiers
Combines uptime, SSL, performance, DNS, and cron monitoring in one dashboard instead of several tools
Built and actively maintained by two well-known developers in the Laravel and PHP community
Transparent, predictable pricing based purely on number of sites monitored
10-day free trial with no credit card required, plus a 30-day money-back guarantee
cons: Pricing is per site or server, so monitoring many small sites can get expensive compared to unlimited-site competitors
Smaller company than large monitoring vendors, so integration breadth may be narrower
No free tier, unlike some competitors offering a limited always-free plan
Primarily English-language interface and support
Focused on website and infrastructure monitoring rather than full application performance monitoring (APM) depth

faqs.question: Who built Oh Dear?
faqs.answer: Oh Dear was founded in 2018 by Freek Van der Herten, a Belgian developer and agency owner, and Mattias Geniar, a sysadmin at the Belgian hosting company Nucleus.

faqs.question: How much does Oh Dear cost?
faqs.answer: Plans start at €15/month for 2 sites (Solo) and scale up based on the number of sites or servers monitored, up to €399/month for 200 sites, with custom Enterprise pricing above that.

faqs.question: Does Oh Dear monitor SSL certificates?
faqs.answer: Yes, SSL certificate expiration and configuration monitoring is included on every plan.

faqs.question: Can Oh Dear monitor cron jobs and scheduled tasks?
faqs.answer: Yes, scheduled task (cron) monitoring is included on every plan, confirming jobs actually run on time.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Oh Dear offers a 10-day free trial with no credit card required, along with a 30-day money-back guarantee.

faqs.question: Does Oh Dear offer status pages?
faqs.answer: Yes, public status pages are included on every plan, letting subscribers follow uptime and incident updates.

faqs.question: What's the difference between Oh Dear's pricing tiers?
faqs.answer: All tiers include the same full feature set; the only difference between plans is the number of sites or servers you can monitor, which increases with each tier.

use_cases.title: Freelance developer client monitoring
use_cases.description: Freelancers monitor uptime, SSL, and performance across several client sites from one dashboard on the Freelance plan.

use_cases.title: Agency-wide site health monitoring
use_cases.description: Digital agencies use Oh Dear's Agency and Portfolio tiers to track uptime, broken links, and performance across dozens of client websites.

use_cases.title: SaaS infrastructure and cron monitoring
use_cases.description: SaaS teams monitor application uptime alongside scheduled background jobs to catch failures that a simple uptime check would miss.
